criteria.label: You can state the formal definition of a matrix, explain what its shape m x n means, and identify rows, columns and individual entries a_ij in concrete examples.
criteria.example: Given a 2 x 3 matrix, you can point out which numbers form row 2, column 3, and entry a_23.

criteria.label: You can determine whether matrix addition, subtraction and scalar multiplication are defined for given matrices and compute the result by hand for small cases.
criteria.example: Given two 2 x 2 matrices, you can quickly write down their sum and 3 times one of them without errors.

criteria.label: You can use the transpose operation deliberately to switch between row-wise and column-wise conventions and to form expressions like x^T y and X^T X with correct shapes.
criteria.example: Given X of shape (batch_size x features), you can decide when to use X and when to use X^T in a derivation and justify your choice.

criteria.label: You can classify special matrices such as identity, diagonal and symmetric matrices, explain why their structure matters, and predict how they behave under addition, scaling and transpose.
criteria.example: You know that the transpose of a diagonal matrix is itself, and that adding two diagonal matrices yields another diagonal matrix.

criteria.label: You can translate between systems of linear equations, matrix equations A x = b, and data/parameter matrices used in simple ML models, and reason about shapes and operations without trial-and-error in code.
criteria.example: Starting from a linear regression model, you can write down its matrix form, identify the shapes of X, w and y, and explain how basic matrix operations correspond to preprocessing and prediction steps.
